metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an unconditional test (not gated on 'if the suite already pins metadata') that introspects each registered tool's annotations and asserts the required hint values, so AC1 and AC2 are machine-verifiable.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command or automated test is specified for AC1 ('every tool carries ToolAnnotations') or AC2 (correct hint values per category). The only testing mention is conditional â€” 'Add/adjust a parity test if the interface suite pins tool metadata' â€” meaning if the suite does not currently pin metadata, no new test will verify annotation presence, leaving the end-state unverifiable by machine.
findings.location: Scope paragraph (test clause) and Acceptance Criteria AC1, AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pytest case in the interface/MCP test file that iterates mcp.list_tools(), asserts every tool has a non-None ToolAnnotations, and spot-checks at least one read tool for readOnlyHint=True and one delete tool for destructiveHint=True.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the minimum `mcp` version at which `ToolAnnotations` and the `annotations=` parameter to `FastMCP.tool` were introduced, and raise the `mcp>=1.2` lower-bound in `pyproject.toml` accordingly (or note the exact import path in the plan so reviewers can confirm it against the declared constraint).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'mcp 1.28.1 exposes ToolAnnotations with all four hint fields and FastMCP.tool accepts annotations' as a confirmed-feasible fact, but pyproject.toml pins the dependency as `mcp>=1.2` â€” far below 1.28.1. No occurrence of `ToolAnnotations` exists anywhere in the codebase, and the import path `mcp.server.fastmcp.ToolAnnotations` (or `mcp.types.ToolAnnotations`) is neither imported nor tested. The plan's stated version (1.28.1) is unverifiable from the repo's minimum constraint, meaning the implementation could fail at import time in any environment satisfying the declared `mcp>=1.2` lower bound.
findings.location: What section â€” 'Confirmed feasible: mcp 1.28.1 exposes ToolAnnotations with all four hint fields and FastMCP.tool accepts annotations'
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml, change `mcp = ["mcp>=1.2"]` and the dev dependency `"mcp>=1.2"` to `mcp>=1.6` (or whichever version introduced ToolAnnotations) and add a smoke-import test: `from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP; from mcp.types import ToolAnnotations` so the CI dependency check catches a version regression.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the exact minimum mcp version that first shipped `ToolAnnotations` and `FastMCP.tool(annotations=...)` support, and bump the `mcp` floor in `pyproject.toml` (both the `[mcp]` optional-dep and the `[dev]` group) to that version.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'mcp 1.28.1 exposes ToolAnnotations with all four hint fields and FastMCP.tool accepts annotations' as the feasibility proof, but pyproject.toml constrains only mcp>=1.2 (both the [mcp] optional-dep and the [dev] group). ToolAnnotations did not exist in all mcp 1.x releases; an installation resolving to an early 1.x version would fail at import or call time with no pin bump to guarantee the feature is present.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section (capability assertion) / pyproject.toml line 50 (`mcp = ["mcp>=1.2"]`) and line 115
findings.suggested_fix: Check the mcp changelog for when ToolAnnotations was introduced (likely ~1.6 based on the 2025-03-26 spec revision), then update pyproject.toml: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.6"]` (or the exact confirmed floor) in both `[project.optional-dependencies]` and `[dev]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Bump the `mcp` lower bound in `pyproject.toml` (both the `[mcp]` optional extra and the `dev` dependency) to the earliest version that exposes `ToolAnnotations` and `FastMCP.tool(annotations=...)` (verify the exact version, likely â‰Ą1.3.x), so the declared constraint matches the confirmed-feasible version (1.28.1) and older installs fail fast at install time rather than at server startup.
findings.finding: The plan confirms feasibility at mcp 1.28.1 but the project's declared lower bound is mcp>=1.2. ToolAnnotations and the FastMCP.tool(annotations=) parameter did not exist before approximately mcp 1.3.x; any environment that satisfies mcp>=1.2 but installs a 1.2.x release will fail at server startup with an ImportError or AttributeError when the new annotation code is reached.
findings.location: What â€” 'Confirmed feasible: mcp 1.28.1 exposes ToolAnnotations'; pyproject.toml lines 50 and 115 (mcp>=1.2)
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml change `mcp>=1.2` â†’ `mcp>=1.3` (or whichever patch version first shipped ToolAnnotations â€” confirm via `python -c 'from mcp.types import ToolAnnotations'` against the target floor). Also add the import path to the plan: `from mcp.types import ToolAnnotations`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the idempotentHint value (true/false) for each named write operation (create, comment, link, edit, claim, transition, set_*) explicitly in the mapping policy rather than relying on 'e.g.' examples.
findings.finding: The idempotentHint classification for the majority of write tools is left to the executor's judgment. The policy says 'idempotentHint: true where the op is idempotent (e.g. tag/untag)' but does not enumerate which of the listed operations (create/comment/link/edit/claim/transition/set_*) are considered idempotent vs non-idempotent, requiring the implementer to make that design call.
findings.location: What / Mapping policy â€” Additive writes bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the mapping policy table to list each write-tool category with its idempotentHint value: e.g. createâ†’false, tag/untagâ†’true, set_priority/set_statusâ†’true (last-write-wins), transitionâ†’false, commentâ†’false, claimâ†’false, linkâ†’false.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make annotation-correctness tests unconditional: add a dedicated test (independent of whether the existing interface suite 'pins' metadata) that asserts every tool in the three registrars carries a ToolAnnotations matching the mapping policy, so a mislabeled destructive tool fails CI immediately.
findings.finding: The plan makes test coverage for annotation correctness conditional ('Add/adjust a parity test *if* the interface suite pins tool metadata'), leaving no guaranteed harness to catch a mislabeled destructive tool (e.g., archive annotated as readOnly). A client relying on these hints would silently skip confirmation flows for a destructive operation, which is the highest-stakes UX failure mode this feature is meant to prevent.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Add/adjust a parity test if the interface suite pins tool metadata.' / AC line 1-2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test in the test suite (e.g., `test_mcp_tool_annotations.py`) that imports all three registrars, collects registered tools, and asserts: tools in `register_read_tools` have `readOnlyHint=True`; tools matching the destructive list (archive/delete/compact/reconcile-live) have `destructiveHint=True`; tools in `register_llm_tools` have `openWorldHint=True`. Make this test unconditional so it runs in CI regardless of pre-existing interface suite behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the mapping policy in 'What' to provide an exhaustive per-tool idempotentHint classification (or an explicit exhaustive rule) for every write tool in `_mcp_writes.py`, not just the `tag/untag` examples.
findings.finding: The mapping policy invokes `idempotentHint: true` for operations that 'are idempotent (e.g. tag/untag)' but never enumerates the complete list. Verified against _mcp_writes.py: at least 12 write tools exist (create_ticket, transition_ticket, claim_ticket, reopen_ticket, comment_ticket, edit_ticket, link_tickets, unlink_tickets, set_file_impact, set_verify_commands, sign_manifest, log_session, compact_ticket, run_workflow) whose idempotentHint classification is left entirely to the executor's inference. This is an undefined-but-referenced decision rule an executor must resolve.
findings.location: What / Mapping policy bullet â€” 'idempotentHint: true where the op is idempotent (e.g. tag/untag)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or bullet list in 'What' covering each write tool: e.g. `tag_ticket`, `untag_ticket`, `set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands` â†’ idempotentHint: true; `create_ticket`, `comment_ticket`, `log_session`, `sign_manifest` â†’ idempotentHint: false; `compact_ticket` â†’ idempotentHint: false (each call reduces the log further); `link_tickets`/`unlink_tickets` â†’ idempotentHint: true (store is convergent).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `fsck` to the mapping policy explicitly, noting that `fsck(recover=True)` is a write operation (should be `readOnlyHint: false, destructiveHint: false` or use the additive-write category) and plain fsck may also remove a lock file, so `readOnlyHint: true` is not appropriate.
findings.finding: The `fsck` tool in `_mcp_reads.py` has conditional write behavior â€” `recover=True` mutates the ticket store, and even plain fsck removes a stale `.git/index.lock` unless `report_only` is set. The plan's mapping policy does not mention `fsck` in any category (not read-only, not additive write, not destructive). An implementer following the registrar-file-to-category rule ('all tools in `_mcp_reads.py` get `readOnlyHint: true`') would annotate it as read-only, which is incorrect.
findings.location: What â€” Mapping policy / src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py: fsck tool (lines 153â€“164)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the annotation mapping policy in an in-repo artifact (e.g., a `CLAUDE.md` section or docstring in a shared helper/constant) so future tool authors have a canonical reference â€” not just the plan document.
findings.finding: The mapping policy (which annotation values each tool category receives) is defined only in the plan document and will be scattered as per-tool inline arguments across three files, with no central reference or documentation update in CLAUDE.md/an ADR. Future developers adding tools have no authoritative, in-repo source of truth for applying the policy consistently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small `_mcp_annotations.py` module (or a section in CLAUDE.md) that states the four-category policy and optionally provides pre-built `ToolAnnotations` constants (e.g., `READ_ONLY_ANNOTATIONS`, `DESTRUCTIVE_ANNOTATIONS`, `OPEN_WORLD_ANNOTATIONS`). Reference it from each registrar, making the policy both discoverable and DRY.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the annotation policy for the unified `reconcile` tool: decide whether to set `destructiveHint: true` (conservative, worst-case) or `destructiveHint: false` with a note that live-mode destruction is gated at call-time, and document this explicitly in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan maps 'reconcile-live' to `destructiveHint: true` as though it is a distinct tool, but the codebase exposes a single `reconcile` tool (in `_mcp_reads.py` at line 257) that accepts a `mode` parameter covering both non-destructive (dry-run/reconcile-check) and destructive (live/bootstrap-strict/bootstrap-throttle) modes at runtime. There is no separate `reconcile_live` tool to annotate.
findings.location: What â€” Mapping policy: 'Destructive/irreversible (archive/delete/compact, reconcile-live)'; Scope: `_mcp_reads.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the mapping policy that the single `reconcile` tool should carry BOTH `destructiveHint: true` AND `openWorldHint: true` (worst-case / safe annotation), since its live modes are both destructive and outward-reaching and a static annotation must cover all modes.
findings.finding: The `reconcile` tool in `_mcp_reads.py` is mode-polymorphic: dry-run is non-destructive and local, but live/bootstrap modes mutate Jira (open-world, destructive). A single static `ToolAnnotations` must cover all modes, yet the plan's mapping policy assigns reconcile-live to the 'destructive' category without saying what annotation the single `reconcile` tool should actually carry. An implementer following the plan could assign only `openWorldHint: true` (matching 'outward-reaching'), omitting `destructiveHint: true`, leaving clients unable to gate confirmation on destructive modes.
findings.location: What â€” Mapping policy / src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py: reconcile tool (line 257)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the mapping policy: 'reconcile carries destructiveHint: true, openWorldHint: true (static worst-case covering its live modes; dry-run is non-destructive but a static hint cannot be mode-conditional)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the exact import statement for `ToolAnnotations` to the plan's 'What' section (e.g., `from mcp.types import ToolAnnotations`) verified against mcp 1.28.1 to remove ambiguity for the implementer.
findings.finding: The plan does not state the import path for ToolAnnotations within the mcp SDK, leaving the implementer to discover it. The correct module (mcp.types vs mcp.server.fastmcp vs mcp.shared.context) varies across SDK versions and is unverified in the plan or any existing code in src/.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'What': 'Import as `from mcp.types import ToolAnnotations` (verified against mcp 1.28.1).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit annotation decision for the `reconcile` tool in the mapping policy, specifying which hint values to use given that it is mode-polymorphic (e.g., 'use the worst-case/most-permissive annotation: readOnlyHint: false, destructiveHint: true, openWorldHint: true, with a docstring note that dry-run is non-mutating').
findings.finding: The `reconcile` tool in `_mcp_reads.py` is mode-polymorphic: dry-run/reconcile-check are read-only and local; bootstrap-strict, bootstrap-throttle, and live modes mutate Jira (open-world, destructive). The plan's mapping policy assigns a single static annotation but does not specify which annotation set to use for this multi-mode tool, nor does it acknowledge the mismatch between tool placement (reads registrar) and its write-capable behavior.
findings.location: What / Mapping policy â€” 'Destructive/irreversible (archive/delete/compact, reconcile-live) -> destructiveHint: true' and 'Outward-reaching (LLM review/verify, Jira reconcile, run_workflow) -> openWorldHint: true'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove 'delete' from the destructive-tool list in the mapping policy (no `delete_ticket` MCP tool exists); confirm the intended coverage is `archive_ticket` + `compact_ticket` only.
findings.finding: The plan's mapping policy cites 'delete' as a destructive tool target (`archive/delete/compact`), but no `delete_ticket` (or any `delete_*`) MCP tool exists in any of the three registrar files â€” only `archive_ticket` and `compact_ticket` are present in `_mcp_writes.py`. 'Delete' is a hallucinated edit target.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the policy bullet to: 'Destructive/irreversible (archive/compact, reconcile-live) â†’ destructiveHint: true'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit and correct the destructive-tool list in the mapping policy: remove 'delete' (no such MCP tool exists), confirm that `compact_ticket` (irreversible event-log compaction) is intended to carry `destructiveHint: true`, and update AC line 2 to reflect the real tool names.
findings.finding: The plan's mapping policy and AC list 'delete' as a tool requiring `destructiveHint: true` ('Destructive/irreversible (archive/delete/compact, reconcile-live)'), but no `delete_ticket` (or any delete-named) tool exists in `_mcp_writes.py`. The complete write-tool inventory in the file is: create_ticket, transition_ticket, claim_ticket, reopen_ticket, comment_ticket, log_session, edit_ticket, link_tickets, unlink_tickets, tag_ticket, untag_ticket, archive_ticket, compact_ticket, set_file_impact, set_verify_commands, sign_manifest, run_workflow.
findings.location: What section â€” Mapping policy: 'Destructive/irreversible (archive/delete/compact, reconcile-live) -> destructiveHint: true'; AC line 2: 'delete/archive/reconcile-live are destructiveHint: true'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC 2) to verify that operations described as idempotent in the mapping policy (e.g. tag/untag) carry `idempotentHint: true`.
findings.finding: The mapping policy in 'What' explicitly assigns `idempotentHint: true` to idempotent operations (e.g. tag/untag), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies idempotentHint is actually applied. This is a described deliverable with no covering criterion.
findings.location: What section (mapping policy) vs. Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 2: 'â€¦; tag/untag and equivalent set-once ops are `idempotentHint: true`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering the parity/metadata test: 'A test asserting per-tool ToolAnnotations values exists (or is updated) and passes in CI.'
findings.finding: The Scope section describes a deliverable ('Add/adjust a parity test if the interface suite pins tool metadata') but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that the parity test was added or updated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC 5: 'A parity test asserting the ToolAnnotations for at least one representative tool per category (read/write/LLM) is present and passes in CI.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
